Q: Worked with e-GMAT to build on a prior Quant foundation, using its concept breakdowns and large question supply.
Supplemented weak math concepts with YouTube and ChatGPT explanations.
Aimed to understand concepts fundamentally rather than just solve isolated problem templates.
Practiced hundreds of variations within each Quant concept and left no topic untouched.
Focused on reading every word carefully to reduce careless errors.
V: Took notes for both CR and RC to stay actively engaged while reading.
For RC, treated passages as stories to explain to someone else, encouraging real interest and comprehension.
Synthesized the main point of each paragraph and asked why the author wrote the passage.
Logged Verbal misses by question topic, original logic, correct logic, logic gap, cause of the gap, and takeaway.
Recognized that Verbal needed repetition but was less straightforward than Quant because more variables affected consistency.
DI: Improved DS from a weakness to 100% official accuracy through diligence and repetition.
Kept math work for each DS statement separate and clearly marked whether each statement was sufficient.
Viewed DI as dependent on core Quant and Verbal skills plus organization.
Found DI hardest to move, with scores stuck in the high 70s to low 80s until late prep.
overall: Improved from a 595 official attempt to 705 after more than a year of total GMAT struggle and four intense final months.
Switched prep providers after eight months of limited progress.
In the final two months, studied three to seven hours per day and did thousands of high-quality reps.
Maintained two logs: one for conceptual gaps and formulas, and one for specific missed questions.
Used every wrong answer to identify section-level weaknesses and personal error patterns.
Reframed study sessions by reconnecting the GMAT to future goals and treating study as an opportunity rather than a burden.
Tried to learn concepts well enough to teach them to a beginner.

Q: Started with a cold official mock to establish baseline weaknesses.
Used TTP's calendar and nearly 300 hours of TTP study to build Quant fundamentals.
Used TTP's bite-sized lessons, practice, and reinforcement as the core Quant resource.
Kept stronger sections in maintenance mode while doubling down on weaker sections.
Maintained an error log for focused practice.
V: As a non-native speaker, used daily focused practice to build Verbal logic and pacing.
Used Marty Murray's CR explanations on GMAT Club as a major Verbal resource.
Bought the Official Verbal Review after identifying Verbal as a weak spot.
Used OG plus GMAT Club explanations to build Verbal confidence.
Let go of CR questions if they dragged beyond about three minutes.
In the final attempt, stayed calm and logical, converting CR and RC practice into V90.
DI: Lost the first attempt largely by spending over six minutes on one Graphs & Tables question.
Improved the retake by using a firm rule to move on if a DI question passed about 2.5 minutes.
Changed order from Quant, DI, break, Verbal to Quant, break, DI, Verbal.
Used OG questions to fine-tune DI because TTP DI tests felt mentally draining.
Used pacing discipline, not new content, as the main DI improvement lever.
overall: Improved from a 615 cold mock to 735 across about four months and two official attempts.
Used TTP, OG, GMAT Club, and official mocks as the final resource rotation.
Trusted official mocks more than third-party scores and treated each official mock as a dress rehearsal.
Booked the exam once mock scores were near target instead of waiting indefinitely.
In the last three to four days, avoided new material and protected mindset.
Used a consistent break routine of restroom, water, and protein bar.
Visualized the target score before the test to reduce anxiety and boost confidence.

Q: Recommended taking an initial mock to establish baseline strengths and weaknesses.
Used official material, GMAT Club explanations, and official mocks as the core resources.
For DS, used an AD/BCE decision framework on paper.
V: For RC, recommended jotting a couple of points per paragraph to create a mental map and answer faster.
Tracked mistakes early and categorized them by concept, logic, careless error, or timing.
DI: Used the same mistake buckets for DI: concept, logic, careless, and timing.
For DS, used an AD/BCE decision framework on paper.
overall: Prepared for about three months while working full-time, usually two to three hours on weekdays and more on weekends.
Believed the first three to four weeks are where many test-takers go wrong by using too many resources and no clear plan.
Recommended a staged prep flow: foundation, timed practice plus weaknesses, then mocks plus decision-making.
Advised not chasing score in the first one to two weeks; instead learn question types and format.
Built improvement through a simple loop: attempt, understand why wrong, and apply the lesson in the next set.
Warned not to exhaust official mocks too quickly because repeated questions reduce their diagnostic value.

Q: Used Target Test Prep's Quant lessons to close knowledge gaps.
Watched every Quant lesson in TTP and credited that as the main improvement driver.
Used TTP question bank, some GMAT Club questions, and many official practice tests.
overall: Prepared for about eight months and around 250 total hours.
Used official practice tests throughout and saw scores range from 625 up to 735 before a 695 official result.
Took Manhattan Prep and Target Test Prep courses, finding TTP more useful because it enforced consistent weekly study.
Found structured course order helpful after earlier scattered study with YouTube one day and random practice the next.
Used the course to recover confidence after a jet-lagged 615 practice test.
Planned a retake because official mocks suggested a higher ceiling, but weighed that against application work.

V: Distinguished casual reading skill from GMAT-specific reading skill.
For RC, focused on passage structure, argument flow, and transition signals rather than passive content absorption.
For hard CR, paused 15-20 seconds after the question to anticipate what the answer should accomplish.
Used pre-analysis to avoid deciding between two attractive answer choices by gut feeling.
Invested four to five minutes upfront to understand RC passages, reducing rereading later.
Took light RC notes focused on keywords, sentence connections, and a one-sentence paragraph summary.
Created abbreviations for technical terms and tracked their function rather than memorizing the term itself.
Stopped relying on more volume with flawed technique and rebuilt the Verbal approach systematically.
Avoided studying Verbal when mentally exhausted after work because poor tired practice distorted confidence.
overall: Improved Verbal from roughly V80-82 plateau to V87 while scoring 685 overall.
Initially used OG practice, then used e-GMAT and Scholaranium for a more structured Verbal approach.
Learned that quality technique with fewer questions beat high-volume practice with bad habits.
Identified recurring loopholes rather than assuming more practice alone would fix the plateau.
